beginner.question: How do you set a background color on an element in CSS?
beginner.answer: Use the background-color property: background-color: #f0f0f0; background-color: blue; or background-color: rgba(255, 0, 0, 0.5);. You can use any valid CSS color format. The background color fills the content, padding, and border areas of the element (but not the margin area, which is always transparent).

beginner.question: How do you add a background image in CSS?
beginner.answer: Use the background-image property with the url() function: background-image: url('image.jpg');. The path in url() is relative to the CSS file, not the HTML file. You can also use multiple images by comma-separating them. Without additional properties, the image will repeat both horizontally and vertically to fill the element.

beginner.question: What are the different values for background-repeat?
beginner.answer: background-repeat controls whether and how a background image tiles. Values are: repeat (default — tiles both horizontally and vertically), repeat-x (tiles horizontally only), repeat-y (tiles vertically only), no-repeat (shows the image once with no tiling), space (tiles without clipping, adding space between tiles), and round (tiles and scales to fit evenly).

beginner.question: What does background-size: cover do?
beginner.answer: background-size: cover scales the background image to be as large as possible while covering the entire element. The image retains its aspect ratio but may be cropped on some sides if the element's proportions don't match the image's proportions. It's commonly used for hero sections and full-screen backgrounds.

beginner.question: What does background-size: contain do?
beginner.answer: background-size: contain scales the background image to be as large as possible while fitting entirely within the element. The entire image is visible with no cropping, but there may be empty space (repeated background color) on some sides if the element's aspect ratio differs from the image's.

beginner.question: How do you position a background image?
beginner.answer: Use background-position to set the starting position of a background image. Values can be keywords (top, bottom, left, right, center), percentages (50% 50% centers the image), or lengths (20px 40px). Two values specify horizontal and vertical position. Single values apply to both axes or set horizontal with vertical defaulting to center.

beginner.question: What is a CSS gradient and how do you create a basic linear gradient?
beginner.answer: A CSS gradient is a smooth transition between two or more colors that replaces the need for gradient images. A basic linear gradient uses linear-gradient(): background: linear-gradient(to right, red, blue);. The first argument specifies the direction (to right, to bottom, 45deg), followed by color stops (at least two).

beginner.question: What is the difference between a linear gradient and a radial gradient?
beginner.answer: A linear gradient transitions colors along a straight line (in a direction like to right or 45deg). A radial gradient transitions colors outward from a center point in a circular or elliptical pattern. Example: radial-gradient(circle, yellow, orange, red) creates a circular gradient from yellow at center to red at edges.

beginner.question: How do you apply multiple background images to a single element?
beginner.answer: Separate multiple backgrounds with commas in the background or background-image property. The first listed image appears on top: background: url('overlay.png') no-repeat center, url('bg.jpg') repeat-x bottom, #f0f0f0;. Each layer can have its own repeat, position, and size values.

beginner.question: Why should you always provide a background-color when using a background-image?
beginner.answer: A background-color serves as a fallback in case the background image fails to load or is still downloading. The color will show through transparent parts of the image and ensures the element has a visible background at all times. It also improves perceived performance — the color appears immediately while the image loads.

intermediate.question: What is the background shorthand and in what order do the values go?
intermediate.answer: The background shorthand is: background: color image repeat position / size attachment clip origin;. Example: background: #fff url('bg.jpg') no-repeat center / cover fixed padding-box content-box;. Not all values are required — omitted values reset to their defaults. The size value must be preceded by a slash (/) after position.

intermediate.question: What does background-attachment: fixed do and how is it used for parallax effects?
intermediate.answer: background-attachment: fixed makes the background image fixed relative to the viewport, so it doesn't scroll with the content. This is used for parallax scrolling effects where the background stays in place while content scrolls over it. Example: .hero { background: url('bg.jpg') center / cover fixed; }. Note: this can cause performance issues on mobile devices.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between background-clip and background-origin?
intermediate.answer: background-origin determines where the background positioning area starts: padding-box (default, starts from the padding edge), border-box (starts from the border edge), or content-box (starts from the content edge). background-clip determines how far the background extends/clips: border-box (default, extends under borders), padding-box (clips at padding edge), content-box (clips at content edge), or text (clips to text shape).

intermediate.question: How do you create a striped pattern using CSS gradients?
intermediate.answer: Use repeating-linear-gradient with hard color stops: background: repeating-linear-gradient(45deg, #000 0px, #000 10px, #fff 10px, #fff 20px); creates diagonal stripes. By placing two stops at the same position, you get a hard edge instead of a smooth transition. For horizontal stripes: repeating-linear-gradient(#000 0px, #000 10px, #fff 10px, #fff 20px).

intermediate.question: How do you create a semi-transparent overlay using background alone?
intermediate.answer: Use a linear-gradient with transparent: background: linear-gradient(rgba(0,0,0,0.5), rgba(0,0,0,0.5)), url('bg.jpg') center / cover;. The gradient overlay is layered on top of the image (since it's listed first). This darkens the image for better text readability without needing a separate overlay element.

intermediate.question: What is the difference between background-size: 100% 100% and cover/contain?
intermediate.answer: background-size: 100% 100% stretches the image to exactly fill the element, ignoring the image's aspect ratio — this distorts the image if the element's proportions differ. cover scales to fill while preserving aspect ratio (cropping if needed). contain scales to fit while preserving aspect ratio (adding gaps if needed). For non-decorative images, avoid 100% 100% as it causes distortion.

intermediate.question: How do background images behave differently when the CSS file is in a different directory than the HTML file?
intermediate.answer: URLs in background-image are resolved relative to the CSS file location, not the HTML file. If your CSS is at /css/style.css and references url('images/bg.jpg'), the browser looks for /css/images/bg.jpg. To reference an image relative to the root, use url('/images/bg.jpg') (starting with /) or use an absolute URL.

intermediate.question: How can you use multiple background images to create a layered visual effect?
intermediate.answer: Layer multiple background images separated by commas, with the top-most layer listed first. Example: background: url('stars.png'), url('moon.png') right top no-repeat, linear-gradient(to bottom, #1a1a2e, #16213e);. Each comma-separated entry can have its own repeat/position/size. This creates complex visuals without extra HTML elements.

intermediate.question: What does background: no-repeat center/cover do and what values are being set?
intermediate.answer: This is an abbreviated background shorthand. It sets background-repeat: no-repeat, background-position: center, and background-size: cover. No background-color or background-image is specified here (they default or inherit). This syntax is commonly used when the background-image is applied separately: background: no-repeat center/cover;

intermediate.question: How do you create a conic gradient and what are its common uses?
intermediate.answer: Conic gradients (conic-gradient()) transition colors around a center point in a circular sweep rather than outward. Syntax: background: conic-gradient(red, yellow, green, blue, red);. Common uses include: color wheels and pickers, pie charts, circular progress indicators, and decorative border effects. The gradient starts at the top (12 o'clock) by default and rotates clockwise.

advanced.question: How does the browser composite multiple background layers and how does z-ordering work?
advanced.answer: Multiple background layers are painted from back to front, meaning the first-listed background appears on top (closest to the viewer). The rendering order is: background-color (at the very bottom), then the last background-image layer, and so on up to the first background-image layer on top. Each layer can have its own clip, origin, and attachment settings, and they're composited using normal alpha blending within the element.

advanced.question: Explain how the background-size: auto and combinations (auto auto, 50% auto, cover, contain) interact with replaced vs non-replaced elements.
advanced.answer: For background-size: auto (default), the image uses its intrinsic dimensions. auto auto uses intrinsic width and height. 50% auto sets width to 50% of the background positioning area and height auto-scales to maintain aspect ratio. On replaced elements (img, video), cover/contain behave differently — the element is sized to its content, not the background. On non-replaced elements, cover/contain relate to the element's background painting area.

advanced.question: How do CSS gradients interact with background-clip: text to create text effects?
advanced.answer: background-clip: text clips the background to only show within the text glyphs. Combined with a gradient: h1 { background: linear-gradient(45deg, #f06, #9f6); -webkit-background-clip: text; -webkit-text-fill-color: transparent; } creates text that shows the gradient through the letters. Text-fill-color must be transparent for the background to show through. The -webkit- prefix is still needed for background-clip: text in many browsers.

advanced.question: How does the background shorthand reset omitted values to their defaults? Give an example of the side effect.
advanced.answer: The background shorthand resets any omitted sub-property to its initial value. Example: if you previously set background-repeat: no-repeat, then write background: url('img.jpg') center, the background-repeat resets to repeat (the default). The image tiles instead of appearing once. To avoid this, either include all needed sub-properties in the shorthand or use individual properties instead.

advanced.question: Explain the performance implications of background-attachment: fixed and how to optimize it.
advanced.answer: background-attachment: fixed forces the browser to repaint the background on every scroll event because it must recalculate the position relative to the viewport. This creates paint work that cannot be GPU-accelerated in some browsers. On mobile, it can cause janky scrolling. Optimizations include: using position: fixed on a separate background element, using will-change: transform, or using a pseudo-element with position: fixed for the background.

advanced.question: How do you create a responsive background image that maintains its aspect ratio as the container sizes change?
advanced.answer: Use padding-bottom percentage trick or aspect-ratio property on the container, then: .container { position: relative; padding-bottom: 56.25%; /* 16:9 aspect ratio */ } .container::before { content: ''; position: absolute; inset: 0; background: url('img.jpg') center / cover no-repeat; }. The padding-bottom creates a proportional height based on width. Alternatively: .container { aspect-ratio: 16/9; background: url('img.jpg') center / cover no-repeat; }.

advanced.question: How do CSS gradient color hints differ from color stops and what visual effect do they create?
advanced.answer: Color hints (also called interpolation hints) are midpoints in a gradient where the interpolation midpoint is shifted. Instead of a color, they specify a percentage: linear-gradient(red 25%, 50%, blue 75%). The 50% hint shifts the color transition midpoint from the default central position to 50% of the way between the two stops. This creates more customized, non-linear color transitions — the gradient eases toward one color before transitioning to the next.

advanced.question: What is the difference between repeating-linear-gradient and linear-gradient with background-size for creating patterns?
advanced.answer: repeating-linear-gradient creates a seamless repeating pattern defined by the color stops: repeating-linear-gradient(red 0px, red 10px, blue 10px, blue 20px) repeats every 20px indefinitely. Using background-size: 20px 20px with a non-repeating linear-gradient achieves a similar visual but with different rendering behavior — repeating-linear-gradient is a single gradient image, while background-size creates a tiled image. repeating-linear-gradient is more performant as it avoids additional tiling computation.

advanced.question: How do you implement a frosted glass (glassmorphism) effect using CSS backgrounds?
advanced.answer: The frosted glass effect uses a semi-transparent background with a backdrop-filter blur: .glass { background: rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.2); backdrop-filter: blur(10px); -webkit-backdrop-filter: blur(10px); border: 1px solid rgba(255, 255, 255, 0.3); }. The backdrop-filter: blur() blurs content behind the element, creating the translucent frosted appearance. A subtle gradient background behind the glass element enhances the effect: background: linear-gradient(135deg, #667eea, #764ba2);.

advanced.question: How does the CSS background-position percentage calculation work (why does 50% 50% center the image)?
advanced.answer: background-position percentages work differently from lengths. A value of X% aligns point X% across the image with point X% across the container. So 0% 0% aligns the image's top-left corner with the container's top-left corner (same as left top). 100% 100% aligns the image's bottom-right with the container's bottom-right (same as right bottom). 50% 50% aligns the image's center with the container's center. The formula: container_position = (container_dimension - image_dimension) * (percentage / 100).
